summary: This is the opening chapter of a dystopian fiction piece set in a near-future where young people have become passive, asexual consumers glued to their phones. The protagonist John, a 19-year-old, reflects on his absent father, his aimless generation, and the sanitized internet before being interrupted by police detectives leaving a cryptic message. The story establishes a world where algorithms have neutered human desire and curiosity, redirecting people away from anything not 'advertiser friendly.' John's father abandoned Wall Street for an off-grid cabin filled with mysterious lab equipment, hinting at larger forces at play.
key_points: Depicts a generation that has lost interest in sex and human connection, reduced to passive scrolling and consumption
The internet has been fully sanitized by algorithms that don't censor but redirect, making transgression nearly impossible
John's father had a 'crisis of conscience' about capitalism and now lives off-grid with suspicious lab equipment
The story contrasts comfort-seeking algorithmic consumption with the anxiety of real-world intrusion
Sets up a mystery involving police interest in John, likely connected to his father's activities
money_quotes: It seemed like the rest of my generation was no longer interested in sex.
They were the agendered mass like the rest of us. Fellow consumers, not providers.
It wasn't actually censored, it would just redirect you elsewhere. And if you didn't pay careful attention, you wouldn't even notice it happening.
Once the algorithms got good enough and it was technically easy to censor, there was nothing holding them back.
I tried asking ChatGPT about hookers and it told me to call them sex workers. And this was kind of triggering. Who the fuck does this machine think it is?
The algorithms got a rise out of me and I went back to comfort food YouTube.
He wanted to talk about 'man shit' like nature and women and not being life's bitch.
key_insight: When algorithms become sophisticated enough to seamlessly redirect rather than overtly censor, they don't just control information—they extinguish desire itself.
